original_hypothesis.title: lockOnBehalf Enables Permanent Fund Freezing via Zero-Cost Unlock Timer Reset
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The _lock function unconditionally overwrites unlockTime to `block.timestamp + lockDuration` for the recipient. Any user can call lockOnBehalf with _quantity=0 (or 1 wei for ETH) for any victim, resetting their unlockTime to the full lock duration from the current timestamp. Repeated calls permanently prevent the victim from ever withdrawing their locked funds.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Directly violates the author-stated invariant 'Funds must not get locked forever.' An attacker can grief any user at near-zero cost on Blast L2, permanently freezing potentially large amounts of locked value.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _lock unconditionally sets `lockedToken.unlockTime = uint32(block.timestamp) + uint32(_lockDuration)` without checking if new unlockTime > old unlockTime for existing locks, and lockOnBehalf permits any caller

preconditions: Victim has tokens locked with quantity > 0
Victim has a non-zero lockDuration set in playerSettings (or lockdrop.minLockDuration > 0)
Token used is configured and active

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: lockOnBehalf allows _quantity=0. For ERC20: allowance >= 0 always passes. transferFrom(attacker, this, 0) succeeds. unlockTime reset to now + victim's lockDuration.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker calls lockOnBehalf(tokenContract, 0, victim) just before victim's unlock time expires. Costs only gas (~0.0001 ETH on Blast). Victim's unlockTime extended by full lockDuration. Repeat indefinitely.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing attack — no direct profit but permanent denial of access to victim's locked funds. Could be weaponized for extortion or competitive sabotage.

economic_questions: Can 0-amount transferFrom succeed for all configured token types?
Does the victim's lockDuration fallback to lockdrop.minLockDuration if unset?
Can an attacker also overwrite a victim's playerSettings.lockDuration via this path?
validation_requirements: PoC: lock 100 ETH as victim, then as attacker call lockOnBehalf(address(0), 0, victim) with msg.value=0
Verify victim cannot unlock after the griefing call
Test with both ETH and ERC20 token paths

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: ~0.0001 ETH per griefing call on Blast L2
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: No direct profit — pure griefing. Victim impact: 100% of their locked balance frozen
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: N/A (griefing); could be monetized via extortion
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single transaction per extension; repeat every lockDuration period

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Just before victim's unlock time expires (maximizes extension per call)
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: No market impact; targeted DoS against specific users

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Any user with locked tokens in the protocol
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire protocol TVL at risk of permanent freezing

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Verify 0-quantity lock path for both ETH and ERC20; check if _onBehalfOf=address(0) falls through to msg.sender
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace unlockTime changes through lockOnBehalf with 0 quantity

validation_result.mechanism: Unconditional unlockTime overwrite via zero-quantity lockOnBehalf
validation_result.impact_summary: Any user can permanently freeze any other user's locked funds by repeatedly calling lockOnBehalf with quantity=0, resetting the victim's unlockTime to block.timestamp + lockDuration at near-zero cost.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The _lock function unconditionally overwrites lockedToken.unlockTime to block.timestamp + _lockDuration on every call, regardless of whether the new unlockTime is before or after the existing unlockTime. The lockOnBehalf function allows any caller to invoke _lock for an arbitrary recipient (_onBehalfOf) with _quantity=0. For ERC20 tokens, allowance >= 0 always holds for uint256, and transferFrom with 0 amount succeeds on standard ERC20 implementations. For ETH, msg.value == 0 matches _quantity == 0. No guard exists that requires _quantity > 0, checks recipient consent, or enforces unlockTime monotonicity. This allows an attacker to repeatedly reset any victim's unlockTime at the cost of only gas, permanently preventing withdrawal. Additionally, the remainder is zeroed on each such call (since remainder variable defaults to 0 when lockdrop is not active), destroying the victim's accumulated NFT remainder.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 311-370 of LockManager.sol confirm the _lock function unconditionally sets unlockTime without comparing to the previous value. Lines 275-293 confirm lockOnBehalf allows any caller to specify any _onBehalfOf address. Lines 323-329 confirm that _quantity=0 passes all value/allowance checks for both ETH and ERC20 paths. Line 353 confirms unlockTime = uint32(block.timestamp) + uint32(_lockDuration) with no comparison to old value.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: unlockTime is unconditionally overwritten to block.timestamp + _lockDuration without checking if it exceeds the previous unlockTime. With _quantity=0, quantity doesn't change but unlockTime is fully reset, extending the victim's lock.
Any caller can set _onBehalfOf to any address (the victim). No minimum quantity check, no recipient consent required. _quantity=0 is accepted.

For ETH: msg.value=0 matches _quantity=0. For ERC20: allowance (uint256) >= 0 always true, and transferFrom(attacker, this, 0) succeeds on standard ERC20 tokens. Zero-quantity locks pass all validation.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker calls lockOnBehalf(configuredERC20Token, 0, victimAddress) with msg.value=0
2. Modifiers pass: notPaused (contract not paused), onlyActiveToken (token is active), onlyConfiguredToken (nftCost != 0), nonReentrant
5. accountManager.getPlayer(victimAddress) returns victim's main account (victim is registered)
6. ERC20 path: msg.value == 0 passes; allowance(attacker, this) >= 0 always true for uint256
7. lockedToken = lockedTokens[victimAddress][token] (victim's existing lock with quantity > 0)
9. If lockdrop not active, lockdrop block is skipped; remainder stays 0
14. lockedToken.unlockTime = block.timestamp + _lockDuration (RESET to full duration from now)
15. Victim's unlockTime extended; repeat before each expiry to permanently freeze funds
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: lockOnBehalf(line 275) → _lock(line 311) → unconditional unlockTime overwrite at line 351-353. No check that new unlockTime >= old unlockTime. No minimum quantity requirement. The attack path is: attacker calls lockOnBehalf with _quantity=0 and _onBehalfOf=victim. The ERC20 allowance check at line 329 passes trivially (0 >= 0). The transferFrom at line 343 transfers 0 tokens. At line 351-353, unlockTime is set to block.timestamp + victim's lockDuration, regardless of the previous unlockTime value.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts directly contradict or support this hypothesis. The configure-token-test.ts shows tokens are configured with active=true and nftCost>0, satisfying the onlyActiveToken and onlyConfiguredToken modifiers. start-lockdrop.ts shows lockdrops are configured with minLockDuration implied by the configuration. The vulnerability exists regardless of specific deployment parameters.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack is fully validated through code tracing. Scenario: (1) Victim locks 100 USDB tokens with 90-day lock duration. unlockTime = T0 + 90 days. (2) At day 89, attacker calls lockOnBehalf(usdbAddress, 0, victimAddress) with msg.value=0. (3) All checks pass: token is active/configured, allowance(attacker)>=0, victim is registered main account. (4) transferFrom(attacker, this, 0) succeeds. (5) victim's unlockTime reset to (T0 + 89 days) + 90 days = T0 + 179 days. (6) Victim cannot unlock for another 90 days. (7) Attacker repeats at day 178. Cost per attack: only gas (~0.0001 ETH on Blast L2). The attack also zeros the victim's remainder as a side effect.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL. Any user's locked funds can be permanently frozen by any other user at near-zero cost. The entire protocol TVL is at risk. The attack also destroys victims' accumulated NFT remainder values. This directly violates the stated invariant that 'Funds must not get locked forever.' The attack is trivially automatable and undetectable without off-chain monitoring.
recommended_mitigations: Require _quantity > 0 in _lock function: add `if (_quantity == 0) revert ZeroQuantityError();`
Enforce unlockTime monotonicity in _lock: `lockedToken.unlockTime = max(lockedToken.unlockTime, uint32(block.timestamp) + uint32(_lockDuration));`
Preserve remainder when locking outside lockdrop: only update remainder if lockdrop is active
Consider requiring recipient consent for lockOnBehalf (e.g., an approval mapping)
Add a separate check that prevents lockOnBehalf from modifying unlockTime for existing locks with quantity > 0
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LockManager with mock AccountManager, ConfigStorage, NFTOverlord. 2. Configure an ERC20 token as active. 3. Register victim account. 4. Victim locks 100e18 tokens with 90-day duration. 5. Warp to day 89. 6. Attacker calls lockOnBehalf(token, 0, victim) with no approval needed. 7. Assert victim's unlockTime is now ~day 179 instead of day 90. 8. Warp to day 90 and verify victim cannot unlock (TokenStillLockedError). 9. Repeat griefing to show permanent freezing.
uncertainties: Whether all configured ERC20 tokens support transferFrom with amount=0 (standard OZ ERC20 does, but exotic tokens may not — though this doesn't prevent the vulnerability, just limits which tokens can be targeted)
Whether accountManager.getPlayer reverts for unregistered recipients (it should, limiting targets to registered users, but this is expected)
Whether accountManager.forceHarvest could revert and block the attack (unlikely as it would also block legitimate locks)
additional_testing_needed: Test with ETH (address(0)) path: lockOnBehalf(address(0), 0, victim) with msg.value=0
Test with ERC20 path: lockOnBehalf(erc20, 0, victim) with no prior approval from attacker
Test remainder destruction: verify victim's remainder is zeroed after griefing call outside lockdrop
Test repeated griefing to confirm indefinite lock extension
Test edge case where victim's lockDuration is 0 and lockdrop.minLockDuration is also 0
